A few days ago I wrote a post about using Cloudflare Access to lock down preview URLs. Once it was deployed, I excitedly shared the URL to LINE for a friend to read — and the share card came back
. Just the default placeholder octocat showing up as the preview thumbnail.

That was weird, because the article has a cover image right at the top, and og:image should've picked it up. I went back to inspect the blog HTML — og:image was pointing at the right cover image, curl-ing the image URL returned 200 OK, but LINE just wouldn't pick it up.
Tried Discord, tried X — same default image everywhere. I started laughing at myself. The article was clearly fine, but every social platform was stuck on stale data.
After some digging, I realized this isn't a blog problem at all — it's
Why do social platforms cache the preview?
Bottom line up front: social platforms
, and a second post of the same URL won't refetch from your site — it just pulls from cache.
OG stands for Open Graph, a metadata spec proposed by Facebook in 2010. It lets social platforms quickly grab a link's title, description, and preview image. Sites just put a few meta tags in
and social platforms pick them up:

<meta property="og:title" content="Article title">
<meta property="og:description" content="Article description">
<meta property="og:image" content="Cover image URL">
<meta property="og:url" content="Page URL">

Think about it: you post a link, 100 friends see that post. The platform isn't going to hit your site 100 times — that'd be 100 requests. So the first time anyone posts the URL, the platform fetches OG metadata once and stores it; everyone else who sees the post (or reposts the same URL later) reads from cache. Your site doesn't get hit again.
That design is reasonable on its own, but when you're a content creator who just updated your og:image and want the preview to refresh, cache becomes the obstacle.

Discord has the shortest cache. Facebook and LinkedIn are on the longer end. LINE / Slack / Telegram have never published official numbers, so when you run into stale previews there, you're stuck using the workaround below.

Facebook is the only platform that still has a working official debugger:

The Sharing Debugger also tells you if og:image has problems (too large, unsupported format, unfetchable, etc.) — really helpful for debugging.
As of 2026, Facebook's official docs still only support

Sometimes the first Scrape Again doesn't work — clicking two or three times in a row often does. There's no rational explanation for this. FB just gets weird sometimes.
X, LinkedIn, LINE, Discord: just use ?v=1

X's Card Validator pulled the preview function in 2022, and the remaining pages keep degrading. LinkedIn has Post Inspector, but it's more limited. LINE, Discord, Slack, Telegram have never offered a debugger.
So for these platforms, the fastest and most universal solution is

These pull your OG meta live, with no relationship to any platform's cache, so they're useful for checking og:image works before publishing. But —

If you're not sure whether your site has a canonical URL, open an article, hit F12 → Elements, and look for
matches the URL you'd expect. Most modern blog frameworks (Hexo, Astro, Next.js, Vite SSG) add it automatically.
One habit I picked up after getting bitten enough times:
run the URL through FB Sharing Debugger before posting
Check that og:image, og:title, og:description are correct.
If they're wrong, fix them on your site and click Scrape Again.
Once everything's clean, share to your social platforms.
It adds a step, but it's way better than posting first, realizing the preview is broken, having to delete the message, having to ask friends to click again — that 1 minute upfront pays for itself.
Social caching is a good thing most of the time (saves your site bandwidth, makes previews snappy), but the moment you need to update og:image, it becomes the obstacle. The
trick feels kind of low-tech, but it genuinely works — especially on debugger-less platforms like LINE and Discord, where it's basically the only option.
Hopefully this gives you a clearer picture of social OG caching and cache-busting. If you're running your blog on Hexo or Cloudflare Pages, my previous post
pairs nicely with this one — together they cover both the deployment-side gotchas and the sharing-side ones. See you in the next post.
